elements.sent-2411-001: Transformer-based diffusion models, dubbed Diffusion Transformers (DiTs), have achieved state-of-the-art performance in image and video generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, their large model size and slow inference speed limit their practical applications, calling for model compression methods such as quantization.
elements.sent-2411-003: Unfortunately, existing DiT quantization methods overlook (1) the impact of reconstruction and (2) the varying quantization sensitivities across different layers, which hinder their achievable performance.
elements.sent-2411-004: To tackle these issues, we propose innovative time-aware quantization for DiTs (TaQ-DiT).
elements.sent-2411-005: Specifically, (1) we observe a non-convergence issue when reconstructing weights and activations separately during quantization and introduce a joint reconstruction method to resolve this problem.
elements.sent-2411-006: To address this, we propose time-variance-aware transformations to facilitate more effective quantization.
elements.sent-2411-007: Experimental results show that when quantizing DiTs' weights to 4-bit and activations to 8-bit (W4A8), our method significantly surpasses previous quantization methods.
elements.sent-2411-008: Recently, inspired by the success of Transformers \cite{ViT,tcsvt-transformer1,tcsvt-transformer2 , Transformer-based DMs dubbed Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have been developed \cite{DiT and exhibit great scalability on more complex generation tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Particularly, the state-of-the-art (SOTA) generation framework Sora \cite{sora is built upon DiTs, highlighting their great potential and effectiveness.}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, their large model size and intensive computations involved in the iterative denoising process result in slow inference speeds \cite{PTQ4DiT , calling for effective model compression methods.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.1cm} \includegraphics[width=1\columnwidth]{figures/DiT_archv3.pdf} \caption{{The architecture of DiT}\cite{DiT}, which consists of several Transformer-based DiT blocks.} \label{DiT_arch}
elements.sent-2411-011: Quantization \cite{ptq4vit , which converts full-precision (FP) parameters into integers, is one of the most effective model compression techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Particularly, Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) has gained significant attention, as it only requires a small calibration dataset and eliminates the expensive weight fine-tuning process \cite{zeroquant,fq-vit,tcsvt-ptq1 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, existing quantization methods are primarily designed for U-Net-based DMs \cite{ptq4dm,ptqd,q-diffusion , leading to performance degradation when directly applied to DiTs due to their unique algorithmic properties and architectures \cite{Q-DiT .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: This underscores the need for specialized quantization algorithms tailored to DiTs.
elements.sent-2411-015: For example, PTQ4DiT \cite{PTQ4DiT identifies the presence of salient channels within DiTs that exhibit extreme magnitudes and variability over timesteps, and proposes dedicated techniques to facilitate quantization.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Additionally, Q-DiT \cite{Q-DiT observes significant variances in both weights and activations within DiTs, and introduces fine-grained quantization with an automatic granularity search strategy to boost performance.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Despite the effectiveness of these methods, they overlook (1) the impact of reconstruction on DiT quantization and (2) the distinct quantization sensitivities of different layers within DiTs.
elements.sent-2411-018: To push the frontier of DiT quantization, we propose TaQ-DiT and make the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-019: We observe that the widely adopted quantization reconstruction method, which optimizes the quantization of weights and activations separately, suffers from non-convergence issues.
elements.sent-2411-020: To address this, we propose using a joint reconstruction approach that integrates the reconstruction of weights and activations to enhance their compatibility and boost quantization performance.
elements.sent-2411-021: We further identify that activations after GELU (Post-GELU activations) within DiTs are particularly sensitive to quantization, due to their (1) significant variability across different denoising steps and (2) extreme asymmetries and variations along the input channel dimension within each step.
elements.sent-2411-022: To address them, we propose time-variance-aware transformations that integrate innovative momentum-base shifting and reconstruction-driven migration techniques to facilitate the quantization of Post-GELU activations with boosted performance.
elements.sent-2411-023: Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of TaQ-DiT.
elements.sent-2411-024: Specifically, TaQ-DiT outperforms the SOTA DiT-dedicated quantization methods by $\downarrow\begin{equation}0.37\end{equation}\sim\begin{equation}\downarrow\end{equation}1.72$ FID (lower is better) when weights are quantized to $4$-bit and activations to $8$-bit (W4A8).
elements.sent-2411-025: As illustrated in Fig. \ref{DiT_arch , DiT consists of $N$ Transformer-based DiT blocks.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Each block consists of two key components: Multi-Head Self-Attention (MHA) and Pointwise Feedforward (PF), both influenced by class conditions and timestep information during the denoising process.
elements.sent-2411-027: Specifically, MHAs are primarily composed of linear projection layers and {matrix multiplications (Matmuls) between queries/keys/values, while each PF consists of two linear layers with a GELU activation function in between.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Therefore, considering both the time-dependent characteristic and the architectural property of Transformer blocks is essential for designing an effective quantization scheme for DiT.
elements.sent-2411-029: Uniform quantization is the most common and easily implementable model compression method, which converts the floating-point parameters $X$ into $b$-bit integer $Q(X)$ via: \begin{equation}\label{uniformquant} Q(X)=\operatorname{clip}\left(\left\lfloor{\frac{X}{S}}\right\rceil+Z, 0, 2^{b}-1\right),\end{equation} where the $S$ and $Z$ represent the scaling factor and zero point, respectively, detailed by: \begin{equation}\label{scale_and_zaro-point} S=\frac{\operatorname{max}(X)-\operatorname{min}(X)}{2^{b}-1}, \ Z=\operatorname{clip}\left(\left\lfloor-\frac{\operatorname{min}(X)}{S}\right\rceil, 0, 2^{b}-1\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-030: Uniform quantization can be applied at different levels of granularity.
elements.sent-2411-031: Specifically, as shown in Fig. \ref{uniform_quant (a), activations typically employ tensor-wise quantization \cite{DiTAS , where a single scaling factor is applied to all elements to ease implementation.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: As elements within each activation token need to be multiplied and summed with elements in the corresponding weight channel, token-wise quantization can also be used for finer granularity \cite{zeroquant .}
elements.sent-2411-033: As depicted in Fig. \ref{uniform_quant (b), it assigns distinct scaling factors to individual activation tokens, thus improving quantization performance without sacrificing hardware efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Similarly, as shown in Fig. \ref{uniform_quant (d), weights often use channel-wise quantization \cite{li2023repq , where each channel is assigned a separate scaling factor, aiming to enhance quantization performance while maintaining hardware efficiency.}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.1cm} \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{figures/uniform_quantv2.pdf} \caption{Illustrating (a) tensor-wise, (b) token-wise, and (c) input-channel-wise quantization for activations, and (d) channel-wise quantization for weights.} \label{uniform_quant}
elements.sent-2411-035: Observation: Non-Convergence Issue in Quantization Reconstruction for Activations.
elements.sent-2411-036: To explore the challenges and opportunities in DiT quantization, we start by applying existing DM quantization approaches \cite{q-diffusion,ptq4vit , which reconstruct scaling factors for weights and activations separately following \cite{brecq .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: However, as listed in Table \ref{tab:reconstruction_method , we observe that quantizing weights to $4$-bit (W4) alone leads to a $\uparrow\begin{equation}3.01$ Fr{\'{e}}chet Inception Distance (FID), while further quantizing activations to $8$-bit (W4A8) results in a significant $\uparrow\end{equation}\mathbf{7.97}$ FID.}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}3.01$ Fr{\'{e}}chet Inception Distance (FID), while further quantizing activations to $8$-bit (W4A8) results in a significant $\uparrow\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-038: This indicates that this widely adopted reconstruction method is unsuitable for DiT quantization, particularly for activations.
elements.sent-2411-039: We attribute this failure to the non-convergence issue during reconstruction.
elements.figure-2411-003: \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2} \caption{Comparisons of different reconstruction methods on DiT \label{tab:reconstruction_method}} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{0.95em} 0.9\linewidth{!}{ \begin{threeparttable} \begin{tabular}{cc|cccc} \Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{\textbf{Method\textsuperscript{*}}} FID$\downarrow$ sFID$\downarrow$ IS$\uparrow$ Precision$\uparrow$ \multicolumn{2}{c|}{\textbf{Full Precision\cite{PTQ4DiT}}} 5.00 19.02 274.78 0.8149 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{\multirow{2}{*}{Separate}} W4 8.01 20.10 183.97 0.7302 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{} W4A8 12.97 25.20 147.58 0.6844 \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Joint} W4A8 6.61 19.50 231.60 0.7713 \Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth} \end{tabular} \begin{tablenotes} \item[*] Tested on 10,000 samples generated by 100 timesteps. \end{tablenotes} \end{threeparttable}} \label{tab:reconstruction}
elements.sent-2411-041: Method: Joint Reconstruction of Both Activations and Weights.
elements.sent-2411-042: To address this issue and boost quantization performance, motivated by QDrop \cite{qdrop , we integrate the reconstruction of weights and activations to facilitate their compatibility: \begin{equation}\label{recon} \min_{S_W, S_A} \mathcal{L} \left[ B(Q(W), Q(A)) - B(W, A) \right],\end{equation} where $B$ represents the blocks or layers to be reconstructed, and $Q(\cdot)$ donates the quantization function.}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{recon} \min_{S_W, S_A} L \left[ B(Q(W), Q(A)) - B(W, A) \right],\end{equation}
elements.subsection-2411-004: Shifting and Migration for Post-GELU Activations
elements.sent-2411-044: As demonstrated in Table \ref{tab:reconstruction_method , despite the effectiveness of joint reconstruction, there still exists a non-negligible performance gap ($\uparrow\begin{equation}1.61$ FID) between the W4A8 quantized model and the full-precision counterpart. To investigate the reason for this, we further conduct ablation studies on the quantization of different layers. As seen in Table \ref{tab:GELU_sensitivity}, PF layers are more sensitive to quantization than attention blocks, leading to even worse performance ($\uparrow\end{equation}\mathbf{2.66}$ FID) than quantizing the entire model.}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}1.61$ FID) between the W4A8 quantized model and the full-precision counterpart. To investigate the reason for this, we further conduct ablation studies on the quantization of different layers. As seen in Table \ref{tab:GELU_sensitivity}, PF layers are more sensitive to quantization than attention blocks, leading to even worse performance ($\uparrow\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-045: Going a step further, we identify that activations after GELU (Post-GELU activations) are the primary contributors to the performance drop in PFs by $\uparrow$$\mathbf{1.47}$ FID.
elements.figure-2411-004: \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2} \caption{Impact of W4A8 quantization on different layers within DiT \label{tab:GELU_sensitivity}} 0.93\linewidth{!}{ \begin{threeparttable} \begin{tabular}{c|cccc} \Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth} Method* FID$\downarrow$ sFID$\downarrow$ IS$\uparrow$ Precision$\uparrow$ \textbf{Full Precision\cite{PTQ4DiT}} 5.00 19.02 274.78 0.8149 \textbf{\lxy{All}} 6.61 19.50 231.60 0.7713 Attentions 5.74 18.67 260.87 0.8066 \textbf{\lxy{PF}s} 7.66 20.69 208.73 0.7497 \textbf{\lxy{PF}s except Post-GELU Activations} 6.19 19.15 234.48 0.7779 \Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth} \end{tabular} \begin{tablenotes} \item[*] Tested on 10,000 samples generated by 100 timesteps. \end{tablenotes} \end{threeparttable}}
elements.sent-2411-046: To address this, we first visualize and analyze Post-GELU activations, then introduce our momentum-based shifting (Method 1) and reconstruction-driven migration (Method 2) to facilitate quantization.
elements.sent-2411-047: Observation 1: Significant Variability Across Different Denoising Steps.
elements.sent-2411-048: To accommodate timestep dependency, two main quantization methods can be considered.
elements.sent-2411-049: The first is dynamic quantization\cite{Q-DiT,smoothquant,zeroquant , which computes unique scaling factors for each timestep during inference, enhancing performance but increasing latency.}
elements.sent-2411-050: The second is static quantization \cite{smoothquant,PTQ4DiT , which relies on aggregated statistics across all timesteps to precompute a single scaling factor during calibration, improving quantization efficiency but often leading to performance degradation.}
elements.figure-2411-005: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.1cm} \includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{figures/time-wise-distrib+lossv4.pdf} \caption{(a)-(c) The trajectories of separate and joint reconstruction during DiT quantization. (d) Activation ranges of Post-GELU activations across different timesteps. } \label{timestep_loss}
elements.sent-2411-051: Observation 2: \lxy{Extreme Asymmetries Within Each Denosing Step.}
elements.sent-2411-052: These irregularities result in the majority of activations having extremely low quantization resolution, thus challenging the vanilla uniform quantization and limiting its achievable performance.
elements.sent-2411-053: For instance, for the Post-GELU activation of the $20^{th}$ block at the $60^{th}$ timestep, when quantizing it to $8$-bit, negative values that constitute $79.7\%$ of the activations can be allocated merely $1.6\%$ of the quantization bins.
elements.sent-2411-054: Additionally, although positive activations are assigned the majority of quantization bins ($98.4\%$), due to the existence of extremely larger values, \lxy{$99.6\%$} of positive values only have \lxy{$16.0\%$} quantization bins.
elements.figure-2411-006: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.1cm} \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{figures/method2-4.pdf} \caption{Distributions and channel-wise ranges of Post-GELU activations, where (a)(d) original, (b)(e) after shifting, and (c)(f) after splitting/migration.} \label{distrib_for_2.1}
elements.sent-2411-055: Method 1: Momentum-Base Shifting for Extreme Asymmetries.
elements.sent-2411-056: Specifically, we subtract activation elements $a^i$ in $i^{th}$ channel by its channel-wise mean (shifting) value $v^i$ to obtain the symmetrized channel $\widetilde{a ^i$ for easing quantization: \begin{equation}\widetilde{a}^i =a^i-v^i, \ \text{where} \label{eq:shifting_activation}\end{equation} \begin{equation}v^i=\frac{\operatorname{max}(a^i)+\operatorname{min}(a^i)}{2}. \label{eq:shiting_value}\end{equation} \shh{To preserve mathematical equivalence while avoiding online computations, the shifting values $V$ can be precomputed with weights $W$ and then merged into the original bias $b$ to form a new bias $\widetilde{b}$: \begin{equation}AW+b=(\widetilde{A}+V)W+b=\widetilde{A}W+(VW+b)=\widetilde{A}W+\widetilde{b},\end{equation} \shh{where $A$ and $\widetilde{A}$ represent the original and shifted activations, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: However, this yields online calculations of shifting values for each denoising step, leading to huge computational and latency overheads.
elements.sent-2411-058: Thus, we propose a momentum-based method to pre-compute shifting values during {calibration : \begin{equation}v_{\text{updated}}=\beta v_{\text{previous}}+(1-\beta)v_{\text{current}}, \label{eq:shiting_update}\end{equation} \shh{where $\beta$ is the momentum coefficient and is set to $0.95$ following \cite{zeroquant .}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Meanwhile, $v_{\text{updated }$ and $v_{\text{previous }$ donate the updated and previous shifting values, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: This implies that rather than directly using Eq. (\ref{eq:shiting_value ) to calculate $v^i$ for shifting activations in Eq. (\ref{eq:shifting_activation ), we use Eq. (\ref{eq:shiting_update ) instead to accommodate temporal variability and enhance quantization performance.}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: For example, in the shifted Post-GELU activation of the $20^{th}$ block at the $60^{th}$ timestep, when quantized to $8$-bit, $79\%$ of the values can now be allocated to $6.8\%$ of the quantization bins, which is $\mathbf{4.25}\times$ larger than before, demonstrating our effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-062: Observation 3: Channel-Wise Variations of Post-GELU Activations.
elements.sent-2411-063: To address this, we take a step further to observe and analyze the distributions of outliers.
elements.sent-2411-064: A natural solution is to adopt channel-wise quantization, which assigns distinct scaling factors to each channel to enhance quantization performance, as shown in Figs.. \ref{uniform_quant (c).}
elements.sent-2411-067: Specifically, after shifting input activations $A$, we identify the top-k channels with the largest ranges of shifted activations $\widetilde{A}$ as outlier channels $\widetilde{a _{o ^{\text{top-k }$, while treating the remaining channels as normal ones $\widetilde{a _{n}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Then, the tensor-wise scaling factor is computed based on $\widetilde{a _{n}$ following Eq. (\ref{scale_and_zaro-point ).}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Subsequently, the channel-wise outlier factor $m^i$ for the $i^{th}$ outlier channel $\widetilde{a _{o ^{i}$ can be calculated as follows: \begin{equation}\label{migration-factor} m^i=\operatorname{round}(\frac{\text{max}(\widetilde{a}_{\text{o}}^i)}{\text{max}(\widetilde{a}_{\text{n}})}). \end{equation} \shh{To incorporate $m$, two methods can be considered.}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: The first involves splitting $\widetilde{a _{o}$ into multiple sub-channels by dividing them by $m$ to mitigate outliers.}
elements.sent-2411-071: However, to maintain the same functionality, the matrix sizes of both activations and weights must be expanded, leading to computational and memory overhead.
elements.sent-2411-072: Despite its hardware efficiency, it may hinder weight quantization and potentially degrade performance.
elements.sent-2411-073: Fortunately, our experimental results demonstrate the robustness of weights to quantization (will be discussed in Sec.
elements.sent-2411-074: To address this while maintaining quantization efficiency, instead of dynamically determining them at each noising step, we optimize them only once during the calibration step.
elements.sent-2411-075: Specifically, we first initialize them using Eq. (\ref{migration-factor ) as a good starting point to ease optimization, then finetune them along with scaling factors using our proposed joint reconstruction method to boost performance.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Dataset: We evaluate TaQ-DiT on the ImageNet dataset at a resolution of $256\begin{equation}\times\end{equation}256$.
elements.sent-2411-077: For the generation process, we set the sampling steps to $100$ and use a Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) score of $1.50$.
elements.sent-2411-078: To construct our calibration dataset, we uniformly select $25$ steps from the total steps and generate $256$ samples at each selected step, then randomly shuffle them across chosen steps.
elements.sent-2411-079: Quantization Settings: We utilize uniform quantization for both activations and weights, with $4$-bit channel-wise quantization for weights and $8$-bit tensor-wise quantization for activations.
elements.sent-2411-080: Our codes are implemented on PyTorch and experimented on NVIDIA RTX A100 GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-081: Baselines: We compare with seven baselines, including (i) PTQ works tailored for U-Net-based DMs, i.e., PTQ4DM \cite{ptq4dm , Q-Diffusion \cite{q-diffusion , PTQD \cite{ptqd , and RepQ \cite{li2023repq , which are applied to DiTs following quantization settings in PTQ4DiT \cite{PTQ4DiT for fair comparisons, as well as (ii) PTQ works dedicated to DiTs, i.e., PTQ4DiT\cite{PTQ4DiT , DiTAS\cite{DiTAS and Q-DiT\cite{Q-DiT .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Metrics: To quantitatively evaluate generated images, we sample $10,000$ images and assess them using four metrics: Fr{\'{e chet Inception Distance (FID), spatial FID (sFID), Inception Score (IS), and Precision.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: All metrics are computed using the ADM toolkit following previous works \cite{DiTAS,PTQ4DiT,Q-DiT .}
elements.figure-2411-007: \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2} \caption{Quantization Performance Comparisons on DiT \label{tab:compare}} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{0.95em} 0.81\linewidth{!}{ \begin{threeparttable} \begin{tabular}{c|ccccc} \Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth} Method FID$\downarrow$ sFID$\downarrow$ IS$\uparrow$ Precision$\uparrow$ \rowcolor{gray!15}Full Precision* 5.00 19.02 274.78 0.8149 \textbf{PTQ4DM* \cite{ptq4dm}} 89.78 57.20 26.02 0.2146 \textbf{Q-Diffusion* \cite{q-diffusion}} 54.95 36.13 42.80 0.3846 \textbf{PTQD* \cite{ptqd}} 55.96 37.24 42.87 0.3948 \textbf{RepQ* \cite{li2023repq}} 26.64 29.42 91.39 0.4347 \textbf{PTQ4DiT* \cite{PTQ4DiT}} 7.75 22.01 190.38 0.7292 \textbf{DiTAS \cite{DiTAS}} 6.86 19.64 218.04 0.7638 \textbf{Q-DiT \cite{Q-DiT}} 6.40 18.60 211.72 0.7609 \rowcolor{dark-green!15} Ours 6.03 18.50 232.36 0.7769 \Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth} \end{tabular} \begin{tablenotes} \item[*] Results are derived from PTQ4DiT. \end{tablenotes} \end{threeparttable}}
elements.figure-2411-008: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.1cm} \includegraphics[width=0.8\columnwidth]{figures/visual.pdf} \caption{Comparisons of generated images between ours and baselines.} \label{visual}
elements.sent-2411-084: Specifically, (1) when compared with quantization methods tailored for U-Net-based DMs, TaQ-DiT outperforms them by a huge margin, e.g., $\downarrow\begin{equation}49.93\end{equation}\sim\begin{equation}\downarrow\end{equation}83.75$ FID.
elements.sent-2411-085: This highlights the need for DiT-specialized quantization algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-086: Besides, (2) TaQ-DiT also exhibits superior results compared to DiT-dedicated quantization baselines.
elements.sent-2411-087: We attribute the improvements to (i) the joint reconstruction method to boost overall performance and (ii) our momentum-based shifting and reconstruction-driven migration to facilitate quantization for sensitive Post-GELU activations.
elements.figure-2411-009: \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2} \caption{The effect of different components proposed in the paper \label{tab:ablation}} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{0.3em} 0.9\linewidth{!}{ \begin{threeparttable} \begin{tabular}{cc|cccc} \Xhline{2\arrayrulewidth} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{Method} \multirow{2}{*}{FID$\downarrow$} \multirow{2}{*}{sFID$\downarrow$} \multirow{2}{*}{IS$\uparrow$} \multirow{2}{*}{Precision$\uparrow$} Joint Reconstruction Shifting + Migration \rowcolor{gray!15}- - 5.00 19.02 274.78 0.8149 {\color{purple}{\xmark}} {\color{purple}{\xmark}} 12.97 25.20 147.58 0.6844 {\color{dark-green}{\cmark}} {\color{purple}{\xmark}} 6.61 19.50 231.60 0.7713 \rowcolor{dark-green!15} {\color{dark-green}{\cmark}} {\color{dark-green}{\cmark}} 6.03 18.50 232.36 0.7769 \Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth} \end{tabular} \end{threeparttable}}
elements.sent-2411-089: As shown in Table \ref{tab:ablation , (1) our {{{joint reconstruction method results in significant performance improvements, e.g., $\downarrow\begin{equation}6.36$ FID and $\uparrow\end{equation}\mathbf{84.02}$ IS.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: By integrating momentum-base shifting and reconstruction-driven migration, as seen in Table \ref{tab:parameters , our time-variance-aware static approach achieves comparable performance ($\downarrow\begin{equation}0.13$ FID and $\downarrow\end{equation}\mathbf{2.69}$ IS) compared to the quantization-inefficient dynamic counterpart, which needs online computations for each denoising step.}
elements.sent-2411-091: This highlights our effectiveness in accommodating temporal variability while enhancing quantization efficiency.
elements.figure-2411-010: \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2} \caption{The method to get shift and split parameters \label{tab:parameters}} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{0.6em} 0.9\linewidth{!}{ \begin{threeparttable} \begin{tabular}{c|cccc} \Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth} Method* FID$\downarrow$ sFID$\downarrow$ IS$\uparrow$ Precision$\uparrow$ \rowcolor{gray!15}\textbf{Full Precision\cite{PTQ4DiT}} 5.00 19.02 274.78 0.8149 Dynamic 6.16 18.74 235.05 0.7840 \rowcolor{dark-green!15} Time-Variance-Aware Static (Ours) 6.03 18.50 232.36 0.7769 \Xhline{3\arrayrulewidth} \end{tabular} \begin{tablenotes} \item[*] The top-2\% channels with the largest ranges of shifted activations are regarded to be split or migrated. \end{tablenotes} \end{threeparttable}}
elements.sent-2411-092: In this paper, we have proposed, developed, and validated TaQ-DiT, an advanced post-training quantization framework for DiTs.
elements.sent-2411-093: First, we propose leveraging joint reconstruction of activations and weights to resolve the non-convergence issue.
elements.sent-2411-094: Then, we develop innovative momentum-based shifting and reconstruction-driven migration techniques to facilitate the effective quantization of sensitive Post-GELU activations.
elements.sent-2411-095: Experimental results validate the effectiveness of our method and show its capability of outperforming state-of-the-art quantization methods and narrowing the gap between quantized and full-precision models.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
